## reference · human-AI interaction 18 guidelines confidence correction feedback expectations

- 18 evidence‑based design guidelines for AI‑enabled experiences, published in a 2019 CHI paper.  
- Guidelines address AI behavior at four moments: initial interaction, during interaction, when the AI is wrong, and over time.  
- Core topics: confidence, correction, feedback, expectations.  
- Each guideline is a concrete design rule (e.g., “Make clear what the system can do”).  
- Guidelines are publicly available at https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/haxtoolkit/ai-guidelines/.  
- The HAX Design Library contains detailed explanations, examples, and design patterns for each guideline.  
- HAX Workbook helps teams prioritize which guidelines to apply and plan implementation.  
- Design patterns exist for 8 of the 18 guidelines; patterns are UI‑independent and reusable across interfaces.  
- Patterns follow a Problem‑Solution‑When‑Use‑How‑Use‑Benefits‑Pitfalls structure.  
- Patterns are intended for early prototyping and requirement definition.  
- Patterns are derived from hundreds of real‑world AI product examples and research synthesis.  
- Guidelines are validated with AI practitioners to reduce ambiguity in AI design.  
- The HAX Toolkit also includes a Playbook, Design Library, and Design Patterns resources.  
- Guidelines cover:  
  1. Make clear what the system can do.  
  2. Make clear how well the system can do what it can do.  
  3. Time services based on context.  
  4. Show contextually relevant information.  
  5. Match relevant social norms.  
  6. Mitigate social biases.  
  7. Support efficient invocation.  
  8. Support efficient dismissal.  
  9. Support efficient correction.  
 10. Scope services when in doubt.  
 11. Make clear why the system did what it did.  
 12. Remember recent interactions.  
 13. Learn from user behavior.  
 14. Update and adapt cautiously.  
 15. Encourage granular feedback.  
 16. Convey the consequences of user actions.  
 17. Provide global controls.  
 18. Notify users about changes.  
- The HAX Toolkit is hosted by Microsoft Research and is part of Microsoft’s broader AI, cloud, and productivity ecosystem.  

Sources: https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/haxtoolkit/ai-guidelines/, https://www.microsoft.com, https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/haxtoolkit/library/, https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/haxtoolkit/design-library-overview/, https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/haxtoolkit/design-patterns/
